meta.title: ICH Q8-Q13 Quality Systems Framework Rules
meta.description: Comprehensive extraction of pharmaceutical quality system requirements, risk management methodology, design space concepts, control strategy requirements, and lifecycle management principles from ICH Q8(R2), Q9(R1), Q10, Q11, Q12, Q13, and Q8/Q9/Q10 Q&As
source_documents: ICH Q9(R1) Quality Risk Management (Step 4, January 2023, corrected January 2025)
ICH Q10 Pharmaceutical Quality System (Step 4, June 2008)
ICH Q11 Development and Manufacture of Drug Substances (Step 4, May 2012)
ICH Q12 Technical and Regulatory Considerations for Pharmaceutical Product Lifecycle Management (Step 4, November 2019)
ICH Q13 Continuous Manufacturing of Drug Substances and Drug Products (Step 4, November 2022)
ICH Q8/Q9/Q10 Q&As (R4/R5) Points to Consider and Concept Papers

foundational_principles.principle: Patient protection through managing risk to quality is of prime importance
Quality should be built in by design, not tested into products
The level of effort, formality, and documentation of quality risk management should be commensurate with the level of risk
Knowledge gained, not volume of data collected, forms the basis for science-based submissions and regulatory evaluations
GMP compliance is always expected regardless of the development approach used
The degree of regulatory flexibility is predicated on the level of relevant scientific knowledge provided in the registration application

objectives.name: Product Realisation
objectives.description: Establish, implement and maintain a system that allows delivery of products with quality attributes appropriate to meet needs of patients, healthcare professionals, and regulatory authorities

objectives.name: State of Control
objectives.description: Develop and use effective monitoring and control systems for process performance and product quality, providing assurance of continued suitability and capability of processes

objectives.name: Continual Improvement
objectives.description: Identify and implement appropriate product quality improvements, process improvements, variability reduction, innovations, and PQS enhancements

lifecycle_stages.name: Technology Transfer
includes: Transfers within or between manufacturing and testing sites

enablers.name: Knowledge Management
enablers.definition: Systematic approach to acquiring, analysing, storing, and disseminating information related to products, manufacturing processes and components

enablers.name: Quality Risk Management
enablers.definition: A systematic process for the assessment, control, communication and review of risks to the quality of the drug product across the product lifecycle

pqs_elements.element: Process Performance and Product Quality Monitoring
key_requirements: Use QRM to establish control strategy
Provide tools for measurement and analysis
Verify continued operation within state of control
Identify sources of variation for continual improvement
Include feedback from internal and external sources
Structured approach to investigation with root cause determination
Level of effort commensurate with level of risk per Q9
Applied to complaints, rejections, non-conformances, recalls, deviations, audit findings
Corrective actions prevent recurrence; preventive actions prevent occurrence
Use QRM to evaluate proposed changes commensurate with risk level
Evaluate changes relative to marketing authorisation and design space
Expert teams from relevant areas must evaluate proposed changes
Post-implementation evaluation to confirm objectives met with no deleterious impact
All changes evaluated by company change management system even if within design space
Senior management responsible for PQS governance
Review inspection results, periodic quality reviews, customer satisfaction
Identify appropriate actions for improvements and resource alignment
Capture and disseminate knowledge from reviews

management_responsibilities: Senior management has ultimate responsibility for effective PQS
Establish quality policy describing overall intentions and direction
Define quality objectives aligned with company strategies
Ensure appropriate communication and escalation processes
Conduct management reviews of process performance and PQS
Independent quality unit with authority required by regional regulations
Ensure processes for control and review of outsourced activities and purchased materials
Define responsibilities when product ownership changes

process_steps.name: Initiation
activities: Define the problem and/or risk question with pertinent assumptions
Assemble background information on potential hazard, harm, or health impact
Specify timeline, deliverables, and level of decision-making

sub_steps.name: Hazard Identification
sub_steps.description: Systematic use of information to identify potential sources of harm. Addresses 'What might go wrong?'
sub_steps.note: Terminology changed from 'Risk Identification' in R1 revision

sub_steps.name: Risk Analysis
sub_steps.description: Estimation of risk associated with identified hazards. Links likelihood of occurrence and severity of harm. May include detectability.
three_questions: What is the likelihood (probability) it will go wrong?

sub_steps.name: Risk Evaluation
sub_steps.description: Compare identified and analyzed risk against given risk criteria. Output can be quantitative or qualitative (high/medium/low).

sub_steps.name: Risk Reduction
sub_steps.description: Focus on mitigation or avoidance when risk exceeds acceptable level. Implementation may introduce new risks requiring reassessment.

sub_steps.name: Risk Acceptance
sub_steps.description: Informed decision to accept residual risk after reduction to an acceptable level, determined case-by-case.

process_steps.name: Risk Communication
process_steps.description: Sharing information about risk and risk management between decision makers and stakeholders at any stage

process_steps.name: Risk Review
process_steps.description: Ongoing monitoring with frequency based on level of risk. Review on planned events (product review, audits) and unplanned events (failures, recalls).

formality_spectrum.description: Formality is a continuum (NOT binary formal/informal)

determining_factors.rule: More important decisions require higher formality and greater need to reduce uncertainty
More complex processes require higher formality

formality_spectrum.critical_rule: Resource constraints must NOT be used to justify lower levels of formality

sources_of_subjectivity: Differences in how risks are assessed
Differences in how hazards, harms, and risks are perceived (bias)
controls: Maximize use of relevant data and knowledge sources
All participants must acknowledge, anticipate, and address subjectivity

decision_making_approaches.description: Formal analysis of available options with in-depth consideration
SOPs, policies based on previously obtained risk understanding

assessment_tools.name: FMEA
assessment_tools.use: Evaluate potential failure modes for processes and their effects on outcomes

assessment_tools.name: FMECA
assessment_tools.full_name: Failure Mode, Effects and Criticality Analysis
assessment_tools.use: Extension of FMEA with severity, probability, and detectability scoring

assessment_tools.name: FTA
assessment_tools.use: Establish pathway to root cause using logical operators (AND/OR)

assessment_tools.name: HACCP
assessment_tools.full_name: Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points
assessment_tools.use: Systematic preventive tool with 7-step process for quality and safety assurance

assessment_tools.name: HAZOP
assessment_tools.use: Brainstorming with guide-words applied to parameters for deviation analysis

assessment_tools.name: PHA
assessment_tools.use: Early-stage analysis based on prior experience, precursor to further studies

assessment_tools.name: Risk Ranking and Filtering
assessment_tools.use: Compare and rank risks with weighting factors or cut-offs

supporting_statistical_tools: Control Charts (Acceptance, CUSUM, Shewhart, Weighted Moving Average)
prohibitions: QRM must NOT obviate obligation to comply with regulatory requirements
QRM must NOT replace appropriate communications between industry and regulators
QRM must NOT be used to justify practices that would otherwise be deemed unacceptable
Risk scores must be based on appropriate use of evidence, science, and knowledge

design_space.definition: The multidimensional combination and interaction of input variables (material attributes) and process parameters that have been demonstrated to provide assurance of quality

core_rules.rule: Working within the design space is NOT considered a change from a regulatory filing perspective
Movement out of the design space IS considered a change and would normally initiate a regulatory post-approval change process
Design space is proposed by the applicant and is subject to regulatory assessment and approval
A combination of proven acceptable ranges based on univariate experimentation does NOT constitute a design space
The entire design space does NOT have to be re-established (e.g., via DOE) at commercial scale
Design space verification should NOT be confused with process validation, though verification can be part of validation
Design space can be updated over the lifecycle as additional knowledge is gained
Near the edges of a design space, risk of excursions is higher due to normal process variation; the control strategy manages this residual risk
An appropriate statistical DOE incorporates a level of confidence that applies to the entire design space, including the edges
Determining the edge of failure is NOT an essential part of establishing a design space

development_approaches: Combination of mechanistic and empirical models
Historical production data for existing products
scope_options: Spanning multiple unit operations (provides more operational flexibility)
Single design space spanning the entire process

rules: Can be developed at any scale; must justify relevance to proposed production scale
Should be described in terms of scale-independent parameters where applicable
Dimensionless numbers and/or models for scaling can be included
Risk-based approach determines verification study design across scales

presentation_formats: Ranges of material attributes and process parameters
Response surface plots and contour plots
Overlapping region of acceptable ranges for multiple CQAs
documentation_requirements: Clear statement of the proposed design space
Description including critical and relevant parameters with ranges or graphical representations
Relationship between inputs (material attributes, process parameters) and CQAs including interactions
Supporting data (prior knowledge, QRM conclusions, experimental studies, design assumptions)
Relationship to other unit operations or process steps
Justification that control strategy maintains process within design space boundaries

control_strategy.definition: A planned set of controls, derived from current product and process understanding, that assures process performance and product quality
elements: Control of input material attributes (drug substance, excipients, primary packaging materials)
Controls implicit in manufacturing process design (e.g., sequence of purification steps)
In-process controls (in-process tests and process parameters)
Controls on drug substance or drug product (e.g., release testing)
Controls for unit operations affecting downstream processing or product quality
In-process or real-time release testing in lieu of end-product testing
Monitoring programs for verifying multivariate prediction models

cqa_control_approaches.description: CQA included on specification and confirmed through testing the final drug substance/product
CQA included on specification and confirmed through upstream controls (e.g., RTRT)
CQA NOT included on specification but ensured through upstream controls

lifecycle_rules: Should be improved over the lifecycle in response to data trends and new knowledge
Continuous process verification enables ongoing monitoring and adjustments
Different control strategies CAN be applied at different sites or with different technologies for the same product
Knowledge management is essential for ongoing effectiveness, especially with contract manufacturing
For contract manufacturing, knowledge transfer must flow in BOTH directions
When multivariate prediction models are used, systems must maintain and update the models
batch_release_elements: System-related data for the current batch (facility, utilities, equipment)
Product-related data from the manufacturing process
Product-related data from quality control (end product testing and/or RTRT)
rtrt_rules: RTR tests are considered specification testing methods
Must follow established regional regulatory requirements for release specifications
Attribute measured must be linked to the CQA and QTPP
RTRT can replace end-product testing but does NOT replace GMP review and quality control steps to release the batch
Not all CQAs need to be included in the specification

qtpp.definition: A prospective summary of the quality characteristics of a drug product that ideally will be achieved to ensure the desired quality, taking into account safety and efficacy
considerations: Intended use in clinical setting, route of administration, dosage form, delivery systems
Therapeutic moiety release or delivery and pharmacokinetic characteristics
Drug product quality criteria (sterility, purity, stability, drug release)

cqa.definition: A physical, chemical, biological, or microbiological property or characteristic that should be within an appropriate limit, range, or distribution to ensure the desired product quality
rules: Generally associated with drug substance, excipients, intermediates, and drug product
Derived from QTPP and/or prior knowledge to guide development
List of potential CQAs can be modified as knowledge and understanding increase
QRM can prioritize the list for subsequent evaluation
Identified through iterative process of risk management and experimentation

criticality_vs_risk.rule: Quality attribute criticality is based on severity of harm and does NOT change as a result of risk management. Risk CAN change. A well-developed control strategy reduces risk but does NOT change criticality.
criticality_vs_risk.process_parameter_criticality: Process parameter criticality IS linked to effect on CQAs and CAN change as a result of risk management because it is based on probability and detectability

minimum_requirements: Define the QTPP as it relates to quality, safety, and efficacy
Identify potential CQAs so product characteristics impacting quality can be studied and controlled
Determine CQAs of drug substance, excipients, and select type/amount for desired quality
enhanced_approach_additional: Systematic evaluation, understanding, and refining of the formulation and manufacturing process
Identify material attributes and process parameters affecting product CQAs through prior knowledge, experimentation, and risk assessment
Determine functional relationships linking material attributes and process parameters to CQAs
Establish design space(s) and/or real-time release testing using enhanced understanding with QRM

overall_development.traditional: Mainly empirical, one variable at a time
overall_development.enhanced: Systematic, mechanistic understanding, multivariate experiments, design space, PAT tools

manufacturing_process.traditional: Fixed, validation based on initial full-scale batches
manufacturing_process.enhanced: Adjustable within design space, lifecycle validation, continuous process verification

process_controls.traditional: In-process tests for go/no-go decisions, off-line analysis
process_controls.enhanced: PAT with feed forward/feedback controls, trending for continual improvement

specifications.traditional: Primary means of control, based on batch data at time of registration
specifications.enhanced: Part of overall quality control strategy, based on desired product performance

traditional.description: Set points and operating ranges based on batch history and univariate experiments, more emphasis on end-product testing
characteristics: Assessment of CQAs primarily at drug substance stage

enhanced.description: Risk management and scientific knowledge used extensively to identify and understand process parameter impacts on CQAs

approaches.rule: Traditional and enhanced approaches are NOT mutually exclusive; combination is acceptable

starting_material_principles.rule: Changes near the beginning of manufacturing have lower potential to impact drug substance quality
Enough of the process must be described to understand impurity formation, fate, and purge and why the control strategy is suitable. Typically includes multiple chemical transformation steps.
Steps impacting the impurity profile should normally be included in Section 3.2.S.2.2
Each branch of a convergent process begins with one or more starting materials. GMP (Q7) applies from first use of starting material.
Starting material should be a substance of defined chemical properties and structure. Non-isolated intermediates are usually not appropriate.
Starting material is incorporated as a significant structural fragment. Commonly available chemicals for salts/esters are reagents, not starting materials.

drug_substance_development.mutagenic_impurity_threshold: 30% of ICH M7 acceptable limit (TTC) is the level above which a mutagenic impurity is considered to impact the impurity profile
process_validation_rules: Process should be validated before commercial distribution
For non-sterile chemical entities, validation results NOT normally included in dossier
For biotech products, data from both commercial-scale and small-scale studies are provided
Number of validation batches depends on complexity, variability, and available knowledge
Continuous process verification (Q8) can be used as alternative to traditional validation

categories.name: Prior Approval (PA)
categories.description: Sufficient risk to require regulatory review and approval PRIOR to implementation

categories.name: Notification (NM/NL)
categories.description: Moderate-to-low risk changes communicated as formal notification within defined period

categories.name: Not Reported (NR)
categories.description: Changes managed and documented within PQS only, verified during inspection

regulatory_tools.definition: Legally binding information considered necessary to assure product quality
rules: Any change to ECs necessitates a submission to regulatory authority
ECs should NOT be confused with CMC regulatory commitments (supportive information)
Identifying ECs for a given product is NOT mandatory unless specified by regulatory requirement
Criticality and risk should be periodically reviewed and ECs/reporting categories updated

identification_approaches.name: Minimal (Traditional)
identification_approaches.result: Large number of ECs with narrower ranges and generally higher reporting categories

identification_approaches.name: Enhanced
identification_approaches.result: Fewer ECs focused on most important parameters, potentially wider ranges and lower reporting categories

identification_approaches.name: Performance-Based
identification_approaches.result: ECs primarily focused on process outputs rather than inputs, enabled by PAT and enhanced control strategy

regulatory_tools.description: Two-step process: (1) Protocol submitted and approved in advance, (2) Execution and reporting per approved categorisation
constraints: Significant unanticipated changes CANNOT be implemented under the protocol
No change should introduce additional risks to patient safety, quality, or efficacy
Changes requiring clinical or non-clinical data NOT suitable for PACMP
If new information indicates increased risk, previously approved reporting category no longer appropriate

regulatory_tools.description: Central repository for ECs, reporting categories, PACMPs, and post-approval commitments

regulatory_tools.tool: Structured Approaches for Analytical Procedure Changes
regulatory_tools.description: 10-step process for managing analytical method changes without prior approval when prerequisites are met
For post-approval changes, stability studies CONFIRM previously approved shelf-life, not establish anew

change_management_principles: Capture stimuli for change including improvements to product/process robustness
Ensure full understanding of scope and implications for all aspects
Leverage existing process performance and product quality knowledge
Require science-based risk management and risk categorisation
Determine data needed and develop study protocols with prospective acceptance criteria
Ensure appropriate regulatory submission when required
Use defined change control process involving Manufacturing, Quality, and Regulatory Affairs
Verify post-implementation that changes effective with no unintended consequences
Capture new knowledge gained and update regulatory filings as needed

continuous_manufacturing.definition: Continuous feeding of input materials into, transformation of in-process materials within, and concomitant removal of output materials from a manufacturing process

modes.name: Hybrid
modes.description: Some unit operations in batch mode, others integrated and continuous

modes.name: Fully Continuous
modes.description: All unit operations integrated and operating in continuous mode

modes.name: Integrated DS/DP
modes.description: Drug substance and drug product operations integrated into a single CM process

batch_definition_rules: ICH Q7 batch definition applies to all CM modes
Batch size can be defined by quantity of output, quantity of input, or run time at defined mass flow rate
Batch size range is permitted if justified
Actual intended size must be defined before manufacturing begins
Changes within approved range managed within PQS; beyond requires regulatory submission

rules: Can be demonstrated even when parameters change within specified ranges and process is not in steady state
Mechanisms must evaluate consistency and identify drift or trends
Root cause of drift should be identified
RTD characterises time for material transport and transformation
RTD is specific to process, composition, material properties, equipment design
Understanding RTD enables material tracking, sampling, and diversion strategies
Must be characterised over planned operating ranges and anticipated input variability
Ability to detect and divert non-conforming material is important CM characteristic
Criteria must be established for triggering start/end of diversion and restart of collection
Diversion strategy must account for impact on material flow and process dynamics
Amount diverted influenced by process dynamics, RTD, severity of disturbances, sampling/diversion locations
PAT is well-suited for CM and enables real-time disturbance detection
CM is readily amenable to automated feedforward/feedback control
Data averaging should use appropriate time intervals rather than entire run time
Assessment of risks from data gaps must inform contingency methods
Model is specific to system design, configuration, and material properties
Acceptance criteria must account for uncertainty in measurement and prediction
Model maintenance and monitoring must occur routinely during commercial manufacture
May need redevelopment and revalidation depending on extent of changes

production_output_changes: Change run time: longer runs may reveal drift, build-up, degradation, fouling
Increase mass flow rates: may impact quality via changes to process dynamics
Scale-out (duplicate equipment): replicate lines follow same control strategy
Scale-up (increase equipment size): RTD and process dynamics may change
rtrt_rules: RTRT is NOT a regulatory requirement for CM implementation
When proposed, associated reference test method must be described
Risk assessment needed for how data collection lapses affect quality decisions
Alternative quality controls must mitigate risks from data lapses

scenarios.action: No diversion needed, no investigation required
Divert non-conforming material per pre-established procedure, concurrent investigation
Monitor closely, divert when cumulative effect exceeds criteria, may pause process, root cause investigation

impact_categories.description: Support development; typically no verification needed
impact_categories.documentation: Discussion of how models were used for process development decisions

impact_categories.description: Useful in assuring quality but not sole indicator
impact_categories.documentation: Model assumptions, summary of inputs/outputs, equations, statistical analysis, comparison with measured data

impact_categories.description: Prediction is significant indicator of product quality (e.g., RTRT models)
impact_categories.documentation: All medium-impact requirements plus sample size justification, data pre-treatment, variable selection rationale, full validation, lifecycle verification approaches

model_governance.rule: The level of oversight must be commensurate with the risk associated with the specific model
lifecycle_requirements: Model validation assesses fitness for intended use based on predetermined acceptance criteria
Verification continues throughout the lifecycle after implementation
Models supporting design space or control strategy at commercial scale must be verified at commercial scale
Validation data sets should account for variability anticipated in future routine production

cross_cutting_rules.rule: The QTPP is the starting point for all development; it describes design criteria and forms the basis for CQAs, CPPs, and control strategy
Criticality of quality attributes does NOT change with risk management; only the level of risk changes. Criticality is based on severity of harm.
Process parameter criticality CAN change with risk management because it is based on probability and detectability
CQAs and CPPs can evolve throughout the product lifecycle
QbD does NOT change regional regulatory requirements but provides opportunities for more flexible approaches
Movement within an approved design space does NOT require a regulatory filing
All changes should be evaluated by a company's change management system even when within design space (PQS perspective)
Knowledge management is bidirectional in contract manufacturing relationships
Overages to compensate for degradation or extend shelf life are discouraged. Any overages must be justified.
Sources of variability that can impact product quality should be identified, appropriately understood, and subsequently controlled
Increased product and process knowledge contributes to a more precise understanding of which post-approval changes require regulatory submission and the appropriate reporting category
An effective PQS and compliance with regional GMPs are necessary to gain full benefit from Q12 lifecycle management tools
Established Conditions are legally binding; any change to them necessitates a submission to the regulatory authority
Quality/manufacturing issues including GMP non-compliance are a significant cause of product availability issues (drug shortages)
For continuous manufacturing, RTRT is NOT a regulatory requirement
The pharmaceutical company is ultimately responsible for ensuring processes are in place to control outsourced activities and purchased materials

glossary.CPP: Critical Process Parameter - a process parameter whose variability has an impact on a critical quality attribute and therefore should be monitored or controlled
glossary.CPV: Continuous Process Verification - an alternative approach to process validation in which manufacturing process performance is continuously monitored and evaluated
glossary.CQA: Critical Quality Attribute - a physical, chemical, biological, or microbiological property or characteristic that should be within an appropriate limit, range, or distribution to ensure desired product quality
glossary.Design Space: The multidimensional combination and interaction of input variables and process parameters that have been demonstrated to provide assurance of quality
glossary.DOE: Design of Experiments - a structured, organized method for determining the relationship between factors affecting a process and its output
glossary.EC: Established Condition - legally binding information considered necessary to assure product quality
glossary.HACCP: Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points
glossary.Knowledge Management: Systematic approach to acquiring, analysing, storing, and disseminating information related to products, manufacturing processes and components
glossary.PAT: Process Analytical Technology - a system for designing, analyzing, and controlling manufacturing through timely measurements of critical quality and performance attributes
glossary.PQS: Pharmaceutical Quality System - management system to direct and control a pharmaceutical company with regard to quality
glossary.Process Robustness: Ability of a process to tolerate variability of materials and changes of the process and equipment without negative impact on quality
glossary.QbD: Quality by Design - a systematic approach to development that begins with predefined objectives and emphasizes product and process understanding and process control
glossary.QRM: Quality Risk Management - a systematic process for the assessment, control, communication and review of risks to quality across the product lifecycle
glossary.QTPP: Quality Target Product Profile - a prospective summary of quality characteristics that ideally will be achieved to ensure desired quality, taking into account safety and efficacy
glossary.RTRT: Real-Time Release Testing - the ability to evaluate and ensure quality of in-process and/or final product based on process data
glossary.RTD: Residence Time Distribution - a measure of the range of residence times experienced by material passing through a specific process environment
glossary.State of Control: A condition in which the set of controls consistently provides assurance of continued process performance and product quality
